summary: Gruber reflects on Apple's decision to abandon Macworld Expo, arguing it reveals Steve Jobs's deep suspicion of tradition itself. He observes that the ritual of Macworld — thousands lining up overnight to watch a CEO announce products — had become so normalized that people forgot how extraordinary it was. He draws a parallel to holiday traditions that seem perfectly normal despite being objectively strange. Quoting John Siracusa's characterization of Apple as 'fearless,' Gruber offers a nuanced reframing: Jobs isn't fearless about change, he's afraid of not changing. The piece suggests that comfort and tradition are enemies of innovation.
key_points: Steve Jobs appears fundamentally antagonistic toward tradition, not just willing to break with it but suspicious of it
Repetition blinds us to how extraordinary certain rituals are — Macworld's overnight lines to watch product announcements had been normalized into seeming ordinary
Apple's uniqueness lies in being both capable of creating powerful traditions and willing to abandon them
The distinction between staying the course and being stuck in a rut is difficult to perceive from inside a tradition
Jobs's disposition is not fearlessness about change but fear of stagnation — an important inversion of the conventional framing
money_quotes: Steve Jobs seems deeply suspicious of, perhaps even outright antagonistic toward, traditions of any sort.

It's hard to imagine any other company abandoning something like Apple's traditional role at Macworld Expo. But, of course, it's even harder to imagine any other company being able to create such a tradition in the first place.
It seems so ugly, the way this has gone down — but there's no way to break with tradition in a nice way.
Repetition blinds us to just how odd certain rituals of tradition are.
Traditions are comforting. But comfort, I think, tends not to breed innovation.
It can be hard to tell whether you're staying the course because it's the right direction, or because you've dug yourself into a deep rut.

It's not that Steve Jobs is fearless, but rather that he's afraid of not changing. Where other CEOs can't bring themselves to do something different, Jobs can't bring himself to keep doing the same thing.
key_insight: Jobs's genius wasn't fearlessness about change — it was a deep fear of stagnation that made him unable to keep doing the same thing, even when the same thing was working.
